![][image1]

**OPPORTUNITY BRIEF**

**Build the visibility layer first**

Prepared for Absolute Mechanical | Lawrence Tweed | June 3, 2026

| 1\. Discovery | 2\. Opportunity | 3\. Pilot | 4\. Next step |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |

| The clearest signal from discovery is that Absolute would benefit from a practical field operations portal: a way for the right person to pull up the current job, building, equipment, drawing, access note, field update, next action, or blocker without calling around. That suggests a different starting point than a broad AI project to mine old records or automate processes. The first step should be a focused scoping effort: translate the operating knowledge now spread across ownership, office operations, and the field into clear requirements; identify the use cases that would matter most; and decide where AI can help clarify, prototype, or improve the system before a build path is chosen. | ![][image2] *Illustrative mobile concept* |
| :---- | :---: |

| RECOMMENDATION Scope a mobile-first field operations portal before pursuing broad AI analysis or automation. | REASON The recurring pain is not missing information; it is that current context is scattered across systems, paper, and memory. | DECISION Pressure-test a scoped pilot with an AI Systems Architect to define workflow, cost, permissions, ownership, and adoption. |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |

|  | CORE IMPLICATION The Client’s interest in a portal, visibility layer, or single pane of glass is the right first area to tackle. Scoping it will require Absolute to map how jobs move, where information lives, which handoffs create friction, and where tacit knowledge has become part of the operating system. That work can surface inefficiencies, duplication, and extra steps while also revealing where later GenAI analysis might mine bids, costs, service history, repairs, and equipment records for patterns that improve decisions and ROI. |
| :---- | :---- |

| 1\. Discovery | 2\. Opportunity | 3\. Pilot | 4\. Next step |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |

**DISCOVERY SIGNALS**

**Different lenses, same visibility problem.**

The discovery responses describe the same business from two different angles: owner/construction visibility and office/service/maintenance execution.

![][image3]

|  | WHY THIS MATTERS The discovery points to a business where useful information is already being created in the course of real work, but it lives across TeamUp, Dropbox, QuickBooks, server folders, paper files, emails, phones, and personal working knowledge. That is why the first opportunity is a visibility and workflow problem: give the office, field, and ownership a shared current view while preserving the flexibility construction work still requires. |
| :---- | :---- |

| 1\. Discovery | 2\. Opportunity | 3\. Pilot | 4\. Next step |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |

**WORKFLOW REALITY**

**One job can touch many systems and handoffs before a clean current view exists.**

The initial call and follow-up responses surfaced the representative workflows below. The exact sequences should be validated with the Absolute team, but the exercise is useful no matter which path is chosen: it shows how work moves across systems, people, and paper records; where inefficiencies or duplicated effort may exist; and what a portal would need to clarify before any build decision.

![][image4]

*The current process reflects a business that has grown around real work, field autonomy, paper habits, service urgency, customer relationships, and accounting controls. The opportunity is to preserve what works while reducing avoidable searching, duplicate entry, and handoff friction.*

| 1\. Discovery | 2\. Opportunity | 3\. Pilot | 4\. Next step |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |

**CONCEPT PREVIEW**

**Use the mobile portal diagram to test the visibility idea before scoping features.**

The diagram below is not a product design. It is a practical prompt for the scoping conversation: what information should a field user, office user, project manager, or owner be able to see from one current operating view?

![][image5]

| 1\. Discovery | 2\. Opportunity | 3\. Pilot | 4\. Next step |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |

**WHAT THE FIELD OPERATIONS PORTAL WOULD DO**

**Create a current operating view without replacing every system first.**

A first version does not need to replace TeamUp, Dropbox, QuickBooks, the server, Microsoft tools, paper files, or field judgment on day one. It could create a cleaner current view while also clarifying which paid apps might eventually be reduced, consolidated, linked, or left alone.

|  | WHAT TO CALL THIS TYPE OF APP Teams often call this kind of tool a field operations portal, work management dashboard, operational visibility layer, or single pane of glass. The common idea is the same: it does not own every record, but it gives the right people one current view of the work. |
| :---- | :---- |

| User group | What they should be able to see or do |
| :---- | :---- |
| Field installer / foreman | Pull up job context from a phone: address, contact, project manager, current drawings, roof access, lockbox notes, equipment list, photos, scope notes, and what needs to be completed. |
| Service technician | See building/equipment history, prior service notes, maintenance history, photos, open recommendations, parts quote needs, and required inspection/startup forms. |
| Office / service manager | See completion status, technician notes/photos, repair proposal needs, parts quote follow-up, preventive maintenance updates, and whether a job is ready for billing review. |
| Ownership | See active work, ownership, status, capacity signals, blockers, and whether relevant building/customer history already exists before someone starts from scratch. |

|  | NOTE ON PARING DOWN APPS The discovery raised the possibility of reducing some systems or subscriptions. That may be possible, but it should follow a source-of-truth review: what each app does today, what information people rely on, what the new portal would replace or merely link to, and what would create risk if removed too early. |
| :---- | :---- |

| 1\. Discovery | 2\. Opportunity | 3\. Pilot | 4\. Next step |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |

**GENERATIVE AI ROLE**

**Use AI to shape the system first; use deeper sensemaking later.**

Generative AI can be useful here as a way to interrogate data, documents, workflow, and tacit knowledge so a business can see useful patterns and use cases it might otherwise miss. In this case, the work begins by studying where information lives, what people repeatedly ask for, which decisions depend on memory, and how AI might help translate that understanding into better systems. Deeper analysis of historical bids, records, and buried data can follow once the operating view is clearer.

| AI-assisted opportunity | How it could help | Guardrail |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Bid invite extraction | Pull GC, GC project manager, job name, address, walkthrough date, and bid due date from emails for review. | Human review before action. |
| Technician notes to draft language | Turn field notes into cleaner customer-facing draft language for office review. | No automatic invoice/proposal sending. |
| Building/equipment history | Summarize approved records so field and office users see relevant context faster. | Only from approved sources. |
| Historical bid/job-cost analysis | Later, study job types, square footage, labor assumptions, equipment patterns, and profitability. | Sequence after records and permissions are clearer. |

|  | RECOMMENDED GUARDRAIL A first phase should not expose QuickBooks Desktop, payroll, banking, margin, customer pricing, or sensitive financial information to AI. AI should assist approved workflows; it should not become the source of truth or automatically communicate with customers. |
| :---- | :---- |

**IMPLEMENTATION PATHS**

**Compare options briefly and practically.**

| Path | Where it may help | Caution / fit |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Off-the-shelf contractor software | May offer dispatch, service history, work orders, forms, inventory, invoicing, and field workflows. | Worth a brief look; do not force construction into a rigid service-dispatch model. |
| Microsoft-based options | Microsoft 365 tools may support controlled records, permissions, lists, forms, and simple mobile workflows. | Current usage appears mostly desktop-based, so adoption and support need scoping. |
| Custom field operations portal | Could be tailored to Absolute's mix of construction, service, preventive maintenance, equipment history, and office review. | Requires disciplined scoping, cost, architecture, maintenance, security, and adoption planning. |

| 1\. Discovery | 2\. Opportunity | 3\. Pilot | 4\. Next step |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |

**ONE POSSIBLE FIRST PILOT**

**Start small enough to learn, not small enough to be irrelevant.**

This is one possible path, not a final recommendation. If the field-operations-portal concept feels right, the next step is a focused pilot that proves whether the idea is useful in real work before rolling anything out broadly.

![][image6]

| 1\. Discovery | 2\. Opportunity | 3\. Pilot | 4\. Next step |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |

**ANTICIPATED QUESTIONS TO RESOLVE**

**The next conversation should answer the practical build questions.**

| Question | Scoping answer to develop |
| :---- | :---- |
| What would this actually look like? | A simple mobile-first job/building/equipment view, not a final product design yet. |
| How much would it cost and who maintains it? | Needs technical scoping around architecture, hosting/security, integrations, pilot size, and support model. |
| What happens to TeamUp, Dropbox, QuickBooks, and Microsoft? | They likely remain in place initially; the pilot decides what to link, standardize, replace, or leave authoritative. |
| How is sensitive information protected? | Use individual credentials, role/job-based permissions, audit logs, and keep QuickBooks integration out of phase one. |
| How does the team actually use it? | Define adoption rules: who updates what, when updates are expected, what old behavior changes, and why the tool helps the field and office. |

| 1\. Discovery | 2\. Opportunity | 3\. Pilot | 4\. Next step |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |

**RECOMMENDED NEXT STEP**

**Use the next call to pressure-test scope, cost, and adoption.**

The goal of the next conversation is not to jump straight into a build. It is to decide whether this field-operations-portal concept is right and what a first pilot would actually require.

|  | SUGGESTED NEXT CONVERSATION If this framing feels accurate, the suggested next step is a scoping call with an AI Systems Architect. That role is useful at this stage because the next questions are technical and operational architecture questions: what a field-operations-portal pilot would require, what it might cost, what should stay out of scope, how security and permissions should work, and whether a custom build, Microsoft-based approach, off-the-shelf option, or hybrid path makes the most sense. The right architect should have experience building bespoke digital platforms and standing up complex operating systems from the ground up, which would make the role a strong steward regardless of which path Absolute ultimately chooses. |
| :---- | :---- |

| Output from scoping | Why it matters |
| :---- | :---- |
| Scoping output | Clarifies target workflow, sample jobs/buildings, required fields, source systems, permission model, likely build path, rough cost range, and operational ownership. |
| Scope boundary | Clarifies what stays out of phase one, especially QuickBooks, payroll, banking, margin, customer pricing, and broad AI analysis. |
| Adoption plan | Explains what the tool is for, who updates it, what old behavior it replaces, and how the team will be supported in using it. |

|  | CHANGE-MANAGEMENT NOTE The change-management piece matters for any process change. Otherwise, the tools a company invests in can sit idle or be used at far less than their maximum value. Any pilot should explain what the tool is for, how it helps, what it replaces, who is responsible for keeping it current, and how the team will know whether it is working. |
| :---- | :---- |

|  | ADDENDUM B: SUPPORTING NOTES / DISCOVERY SYNTHESIS A supporting memo is included separately for additional discovery context. It is not a second brief; it preserves the logic behind the recommendation so the main brief can stay focused on the practical next step: scope the field operations portal first, then choose the right path. |
| :---- | :---- |
